approaches.name: Brute Force O(N*M)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all integers up to the product of moduli, checking each against all congruences until a solution is found. Use this for small inputs or as a baseline to understand the problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Solution to Chinese Remainder Theorem
ladders.desc: Find the smallest non-negative integer satisfying multiple congruences by checking all numbers up to the product of moduli.
ladders.explanation: The Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) states that given pairwise coprime moduli, there exists a unique solution modulo their product. The brute force approach doesn't require coprimality but simply checks every integer from 0 to M-1 (where M is the product of all moduli) to find one satisfying all congruences. Intuition: Since any solution must be congruent modulo each modulus, we can exhaustively search the smallest possible solution space. The algorithm: 1) Compute product M of all moduli. 2) For x from 0 to M-1: check if x % m_i == a_i for all i. 3) Return first x satisfying all, or indicate no solution. Time: O(N*M) where N is number of congruences, M is product of moduli. Space: O(1). Connection: This is the complete implementation of the approach. Common mistakes: Not handling non-coprime moduli (this approach works but may find no solution), not checking up to M-1 (solutions repeat modulo M), inefficient for large moduli. Real pattern: Iterate through candidate solutions, validate against all constraints.

testCases.input: x ≡ 2 mod 3, x ≡ 3 mod 4, x ≡ 1 mod 5
x ≡ 2 mod 3, x ≡ 3 mod 5, x ≡ 2 mod 7

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Validate Candidate Against All Congruences
ladders.desc: Check if a given integer satisfies all congruence conditions simultaneously.
ladders.explanation: This is the core validation step in the brute force approach. Given an integer x and a list of congruences (each with remainder a_i and modulus m_i), we need to verify that x % m_i == a_i for every i. Intuition: A solution must satisfy each congruence individually; this function aggregates those checks. Algorithm: 1) For each congruence (a_i, m_i): compute x % m_i. 2) If any result ≠ a_i, return False. 3) If all match, return True. Time: O(N) where N is number of congruences. Space: O(1). Connection: This function is called for each candidate x in the full solution. Common mistakes: Using integer division instead of modulo, not handling negative x (should use modulo properly), early exit on first mismatch. Real pattern: Loop through constraints, validate each.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check first: 8 % 3 = 2, matches 2. Step 2: Check second: 8 % 5 = 3, matches 3. All match, return True.
Step 1: Check first: 7 % 2 = 1, matches 1. Step 2: Check second: 7 % 4 = 3, does not match 2. Mismatch found, return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Search Through Solution Space
ladders.desc: Systematically check integers in a range to find one meeting all conditions.
ladders.explanation: The central technique is exhaustive search: generate candidate solutions in order and test each. Intuition: Since solutions are periodic with period equal to the least common multiple of moduli (or product for brute force), we only need to check a finite range. For brute force, range is 0 to M-1 where M is product of moduli. Algorithm: 1) Compute M = product of all m_i. 2) For x from 0 to M-1: if x satisfies all congruences (using validation), return x. 3) If loop ends, no solution. Time: O(M*N) worst-case. Space: O(1). Connection: This implements the search loop that calls the validation subroutine. Common mistakes: Starting from 1 instead of 0 (missing solution x=0), going beyond M-1 (unnecessary), not handling no-solution case. Real pattern: For-loop with early return on success.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Product of Moduli
ladders.desc: Calculate the product of all modulus values from the congruences.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate step determines the upper bound for the brute force search. Intuition: Any solution to the congruences will repeat with period equal to the least common multiple of the moduli; for brute force, we use the product as a safe upper bound (since LCM ≤ product). Algorithm: 1) Initialize product = 1. 2) For each modulus m_i: product *= m_i. 3) Return product. Time: O(N). Space: O(1). Connection: This product M is used in the iterative search to define the range 0 to M-1. Common mistakes: Starting product at 0, integer overflow (though for brute force, large products make approach impractical), not handling empty input. Real pattern: Accumulate product via multiplication.

examples.trace: Step 1: product=1. Step 2: Multiply by 3: product=1*3=3. Step 3: Multiply by 5: product=3*5=15. Return 15.
Step 1: product=1. Step 2: Multiply by 2: product=1*2=2. Step 3: Multiply by 4: product=2*4=8. Return 8.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Operation Check
ladders.desc: Verify if two numbers are congruent modulo a given modulus.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation checks whether a ≡ b (mod m), i.e., whether a % m equals b % m. In our context, we check if x % m equals given remainder a. Intuition: Congruence means same remainder when divided by m. Algorithm: Compute x % m, compare to a. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection: This is the atomic check used in the validation subroutine for each congruence. Common mistakes: For negative x, need proper modulo (e.g., in Python, -1 % 3 = 2), confusing with division, off-by-one errors. Real pattern: Use % operator and compare.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute 8 % 3 = 2. Step 2: Compare 2 == 2. True.
Step 1: Compute 7 % 4 = 3. Step 2: Compare 3 == 2. False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Congruence Modulo
ladders.desc: Grasp the basic idea that two numbers have the same remainder when divided by a modulus.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: for integers a, b, and m (m > 0), a ≡ b (mod m) means m divides (a - b), or equivalently, a % m = b % m. Intuition: Think of clock arithmetic: on a 12-hour clock, 15 ≡ 3 (mod 12) because both represent 3 o'clock. Step-by-step: 1) Choose modulus m (e.g., 5). 2) For any integer, compute remainder when divided by m (e.g., 13 % 5 = 3). 3) Numbers with same remainder are congruent (e.g., 3, 8, 13 are all ≡ 3 mod 5). Time/space: O(1) for understanding. Connection: This defines what a congruence equation means, forming the basis for all higher levels. Common mistakes: Thinking congruence means equality, not understanding remainder computation, mixing up dividend and divisor. Real pattern: Use modulo operator to find remainder.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute 17 % 6 = 5. Step 2: Compute 5 % 6 = 5. Step 3: Both remainders are 5, so they are congruent modulo 6.
Step 1: Compute 10 % 4 = 2. Step 2: Compute 3 % 4 = 3. Step 3: Remainders 2 and 3 are different, so not congruent modulo 4.

approaches.name: Extended Euclidean Algorithm O(N log M)
approaches.summary: Solve pairwise congruences by combining them using the extended Euclidean algorithm to find modular inverses and merge solutions. This is the standard method for solving systems of congruences with pairwise coprime moduli.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Solve System of Congruences Using Extended Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Given N congruences x ≡ a_i (mod m_i) with pairwise coprime moduli, find the smallest non-negative solution using pairwise combination via extended Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) states that for pairwise coprime moduli m_i, there exists a unique solution modulo M = ∏m_i. The extended Euclidean algorithm approach solves this by iteratively combining congruences. Intuition: We can merge two congruences x ≡ a1 (mod m1) and x ≡ a2 (mod m2) by finding integers that satisfy both. This is done by expressing x as a1 + m1*k and requiring it to be congruent to a2 modulo m2. This gives m1*k ≡ (a2 - a1) (mod m2). Since m1 and m2 are coprime, m1 has a modular inverse modulo m2, which we find using the extended Euclidean algorithm. Let inv = modular_inverse(m1, m2), then k ≡ (a2 - a1)*inv (mod m2). So k = (a2 - a1)*inv mod m2, and x = a1 + m1*k. This new x satisfies both congruences modulo lcm(m1,m2) = m1*m2 (since coprime). We repeat this process for all congruences, merging them pairwise. Time complexity: O(N log M) where M is the product of moduli, since each merge involves extended Euclidean algorithm which is O(log min(m_i, m_j)). Space: O(1) for iterative combination. Connection to L1: This uses the merge_two_congruences subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes: Not handling negative differences properly (use modulo arithmetic), forgetting to take result modulo new modulus, assuming moduli are pairwise coprime without verification. Real code pattern: Initialize solution = a1, modulus = m1. For each subsequent congruence (a_i, m_i): solution = merge(solution, modulus, a_i, m_i); modulus *= m_i. Return solution mod modulus.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with first congruence: x ≡ 2 (mod 3), so current solution = 2, current modulus = 3.
Step 2: Merge with x ≡ 3 (mod 5): Compute difference = 3 - 2 = 1. Find modular inverse of 3 modulo 5: 3*2 ≡ 6 ≡ 1 (mod 5), so inv = 2. Compute k = (1 * 2) mod 5 = 2. New solution = 2 + 3*2 = 8. New modulus = 3*5 = 15.
Step 3: Merge with x ≡ 2 (mod 7): Current solution = 8, current modulus = 15. Difference = 2 - 8 = -6 ≡ 1 (mod 7) since -6 mod 7 = 1. Find modular inverse of 15 modulo 7: 15 ≡ 1 (mod 7), so inv = 1 (since 1*1 ≡ 1 mod 7). Compute k = (1 * 1) mod 7 = 1. New solution = 8 + 15*1 = 23. New modulus = 15*7 = 105.
Step 4: Final solution is 23 mod 105 = 23 (smallest non-negative).

Step 1: Start: solution = 1, modulus = 2.
Step 2: Merge with x ≡ 2 (mod 3): Difference = 2 - 1 = 1. Modular inverse of 2 mod 3: 2*2 ≡ 4 ≡ 1 (mod 3), so inv = 2. k = (1 * 2) mod 3 = 2. New solution = 1 + 2*2 = 5. New modulus = 2*3 = 6.
Step 3: Merge with x ≡ 3 (mod 5): Difference = 3 - 5 = -2 ≡ 3 (mod 5) since -2 mod 5 = 3. Modular inverse of 6 mod 5: 6 ≡ 1 (mod 5), so inv = 1. k = (3 * 1) mod 5 = 3. New solution = 5 + 6*3 = 23. New modulus = 6*5 = 30.
Step 4: Final solution = 23 mod 30 = 23.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Two Congruences Using Modular Inverse
ladders.desc: Given two congruences x ≡ a1 (mod m1) and x ≡ a2 (mod m2) with coprime m1,m2, find x satisfying both modulo m1*m2.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core operation in CRT solution. Intuition: We need to find x that is simultaneously a1 mod m1 and a2 mod m2. Express x as a1 + m1*k. Then we require a1 + m1*k ≡ a2 (mod m2), which simplifies to m1*k ≡ (a2 - a1) (mod m2). Since m1 and m2 are coprime, m1 has a modular inverse modulo m2 (found via extended Euclidean algorithm). Let inv = modular_inverse(m1, m2). Then k ≡ (a2 - a1)*inv (mod m2). Compute k = ((a2 - a1) * inv) mod m2 (handling negative differences properly using modulo). Then x = a1 + m1*k. This x satisfies both congruences. The new modulus is m1*m2 (since lcm(m1,m2) = m1*m2 for coprime numbers). Time: O(log min(m1,m2)) for extended Euclidean algorithm. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called repeatedly in the full solution. Common mistakes: Not reducing (a2 - a1) modulo m2 before multiplication, forgetting to take k modulo m2, incorrect handling when difference is negative. Real code pattern: def merge_two(a1, m1, a2, m2): diff = (a2 - a1) % m2; inv = mod_inverse(m1, m2); k = (diff * inv) % m2; x = a1 + m1 * k; return x % (m1 * m2).

examples.trace:
Step 1: a1=2, m1=3, a2=3, m2=5.
Step 2: Compute difference = (3 - 2) % 5 = 1 % 5 = 1.
Step 3: Find modular inverse of 3 modulo 5: Solve 3*inv ≡ 1 (mod 5). Try inv=2: 3*2=6 ≡ 1 mod 5. So inv=2.
Step 4: Compute k = (1 * 2) % 5 = 2 % 5 = 2.
Step 5: Compute x = 2 + 3*2 = 8.
Step 6: Verify: 8 mod 3 = 2, 8 mod 5 = 3. Correct.

Step 1: a1=1, m1=4, a2=3, m2=5.
Step 2: Difference = (3 - 1) % 5 = 2 % 5 = 2.
Step 3: Modular inverse of 4 mod 5: 4*4=16 ≡ 1 mod 5, so inv=4.
Step 4: k = (2 * 4) % 5 = 8 % 5 = 3.
Step 5: x = 1 + 4*3 = 13.
Step 6: Reduce modulo 4*5=20: 13 mod 20 = 13. But verify: 13 mod 4 = 1, 13 mod 5 = 3. Actually 13 works, but smallest non-negative is 13. Wait, check: 13 mod 20 = 13. But let's see if smaller: 13-20=-7 not non-negative. So output is 13. Correction: In example, output should be 13. Let me recompute: x=13. Verify: 13 mod 4 = 1, 13 mod 5 = 3. Yes. So output 13. But in trace I said 11 earlier, that was error. Correct output is 13.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Find Modular Inverse Using Extended Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Given integers a and m with gcd(a,m)=1, find integer inv such that a*inv ≡ 1 (mod m).
ladders.explanation: The modular inverse is crucial for solving linear congruences. Intuition: We need inv where a*inv = 1 + m*k for some integer k. This is equivalent to a*inv - m*k = 1, which is a linear Diophantine equation. The extended Euclidean algorithm finds integers x,y such that a*x + m*y = gcd(a,m). Since gcd(a,m)=1, we get a*x + m*y = 1. Taking modulo m, a*x ≡ 1 (mod m), so x is the modular inverse (may need adjustment to be in range 0..m-1). Steps: 1) Use extended Euclidean algorithm to compute gcd and coefficients x,y. 2) The coefficient x for a is a candidate inverse. 3) Reduce x modulo m to get inv in [0,m-1]. Time: O(log min(a,m)) via Euclidean algorithm. Space: O(1) for iterative version. Connection to L1: This is used in merge_two to solve m1*k ≡ diff (mod m2). Common mistakes: Not handling negative x (add m until positive), assuming inverse exists without checking gcd=1, using integer division incorrectly. Real code pattern: def mod_inverse(a, m): g, x, y = extended_gcd(a, m); if g != 1: return None (no inverse); else: return x % m.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Use extended Euclidean algorithm for 3 and 5.
Initialize: a=3, b=5.
Step 2: 5 = 1*3 + 2, so quotient q=1, remainder r=2.
Step 3: 3 = 1*2 + 1, q=1, r=1.
Step 4: 2 = 2*1 + 0, r=0, stop.
Back-substitute to find coefficients:
From step 3: 1 = 3 - 1*2.
From step 2: 2 = 5 - 1*3.
Substitute: 1 = 3 - 1*(5 - 1*3) = 3 - 5 + 3 = 2*3 - 1*5.
So coefficients: x=2 for 3, y=-1 for 5.
Step 5: Modular inverse is x mod m = 2 mod 5 = 2.
Verify: 3*2 = 6 ≡ 1 mod 5.

Step 1: Extended Euclidean for 4 and 7.
7 = 1*4 + 3, q=1, r=3.
4 = 1*3 + 1, q=1, r=1.
3 = 3*1 + 0, r=0.
Back-substitute:
From 4 = 1*3 + 1: 1 = 4 - 1*3.
From 7 = 1*4 + 3: 3 = 7 - 1*4.
Substitute: 1 = 4 - 1*(7 - 1*4) = 4 - 7 + 4 = 2*4 - 1*7.
So x=2, y=-1.
Modular inverse = 2 mod 7 = 2.
Verify: 4*2=8 ≡ 1 mod 7.

ladders.title: Building Block — Extended Euclidean Algorithm for GCD and Coefficients
ladders.desc: Given integers a,b, find gcd(a,b) and integers x,y such that a*x + b*y = gcd(a,b).
ladders.explanation: The extended Euclidean algorithm computes not only the greatest common divisor but also Bézout coefficients x,y. Intuition: It extends the standard Euclidean algorithm by keeping track of how the remainders can be expressed as linear combinations of a and b. At each step, we have remainders r_i and coefficients x_i,y_i such that r_i = a*x_i + b*y_i. Initially, r0=a with (x0,y0)=(1,0), r1=b with (x1,y1)=(0,1). At step i, compute quotient q = r_{i-1} // r_i and remainder r_{i+1} = r_{i-1} - q*r_i. Update coefficients: x_{i+1} = x_{i-1} - q*x_i, y_{i+1} = y_{i-1} - q*y_i. Repeat until r_i=0, then gcd = r_{i-1}, with coefficients (x_{i-1}, y_{i-1}). Time: O(log min(a,b)) same as Euclidean algorithm. Space: O(1) for iterative version. Connection to L2: This is used to find modular inverse by solving a*x + m*y = 1. Common mistakes: Incorrect sign handling, not updating coefficients properly, using integer division incorrectly. Real code pattern: def extended_gcd(a, b): old_r, r = a, b; old_s, s = 1, 0; old_t, t = 0, 1; while r != 0: q = old_r // r; old_r, r = r, old_r - q*r; old_s, s = s, old_s - q*s; old_t, t = t, old_t - q*t; return old_r, old_s, old_t.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize: old_r=30, r=20; old_s=1, s=0; old_t=0, t=1.
Step 2: q = 30 // 20 = 1. Update: new_r = 30 - 1*20 = 10. So old_r=20, r=10. new_s = 1 - 1*0 = 1, so old_s=0, s=1. new_t = 0 - 1*1 = -1, so old_t=1, t=-1.
Step 3: q = 20 // 10 = 2. Update: new_r = 20 - 2*10 = 0. So old_r=10, r=0. new_s = 0 - 2*1 = -2, so old_s=1, s=-2. new_t = 1 - 2*(-1) = 3, so old_t=-1, t=3.
Step 4: r=0, stop. gcd = old_r = 10. Coefficients: x = old_s = 1, y = old_t = -1.
Verify: 30*1 + 20*(-1) = 30 - 20 = 10 = gcd.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Euclidean Algorithm for Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)
ladders.desc: Given two integers a and b, find their greatest common divisor using the Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The Euclidean algorithm is based on the principle that gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b). Intuition: If d divides both a and b, then it also divides a - q*b for any integer q. The remainder a mod b is such a combination. By repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder, we reduce the numbers until the remainder is 0, at which point the other number is the gcd. Steps: 1) While b ≠ 0: set a, b = b, a % b. 2) Return a. Time: O(log min(a,b)) because the numbers reduce quickly (at least by factor ~1.618 per step). Space: O(1) for iterative version. Connection to L3: This is the foundation for extended Euclidean algorithm. Common mistakes: Not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loop if not updating variables correctly, confusion with modulo operation. Real code pattern: def gcd(a, b): while b: a, b = b, a % b; return abs(a).

examples.trace:
Step 1: a=48, b=18. Compute a % b = 48 % 18 = 12. Update: a=18, b=12.
Step 2: a=18, b=12. Compute 18 % 12 = 6. Update: a=12, b=6.
Step 3: a=12, b=6. Compute 12 % 6 = 0. Update: a=6, b=0.
Step 4: b=0, stop. gcd = a = 6.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Modular Arithmetic Basics
ladders.desc: Understand congruence modulo n: a ≡ b (mod n) means n divides (a-b).
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic is arithmetic on remainders. Intuition: Two numbers are congruent modulo n if they have the same remainder when divided by n. Formally, a ≡ b (mod n) iff n | (a-b). This defines equivalence classes modulo n. Key properties: 1) Addition: If a ≡ b (mod n) and c ≡ d (mod n), then a+c ≡ b+d (mod n). 2) Multiplication: a*c ≡ b*d (mod n). 3) Exponentiation: a^k ≡ b^k (mod n). The modulo operation a mod n gives the remainder in [0,n-1]. Negative numbers: -a mod n = n - (a mod n) if a mod n ≠ 0, else 0. Time: O(1) for basic operations. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: GCD and modular inverses rely on congruence concepts. Common mistakes: Confusing congruence with equality, mishandling negative numbers in modulo, not reducing numbers modulo n before operations. Real code pattern: In Python, use % operator: remainder = a % n (gives result in [0,n-1]). For negative a, Python's % gives non-negative result, e.g., -1 % 5 = 4.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute difference: 17 - 5 = 12.
Step 2: Check if 6 divides 12: 12 / 6 = 2, remainder 0.
Step 3: Since 6 | 12, 17 ≡ 5 (mod 6).
Alternative: Compute remainders: 17 mod 6 = 5, 5 mod 6 = 5. Same remainder, so congruent.

Step 1: For negative number, find smallest non-negative remainder.
Step 2: Add multiples of 7 until non-negative: -3 + 7 = 4.
Step 3: So -3 mod 7 = 4.
Verify: 4 ≡ -3 (mod 7) because 4 - (-3) = 7, which is divisible by 7.

approaches.name: Garner's Algorithm O(N² log M)
approaches.summary: Convert the solution to mixed radix representation using Garner's algorithm, which builds the solution incrementally without requiring moduli to be coprime. This is useful for non-coprime moduli or when intermediate results need to be controlled.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Solve System of Congruences Using Garner's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Given N congruences x ≡ a_i (mod m_i), find the smallest non-negative solution x using Garner's algorithm without requiring coprime moduli.
ladders.explanation: Garner's algorithm solves systems of congruences by converting the solution to mixed radix representation, building it incrementally. Unlike the Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT), it doesn't require moduli to be coprime, making it more general. The intuition is to express x as x = v_0 + v_1*m_0 + v_2*m_0*m_1 + ... + v_{N-1}*m_0*m_1*...*m_{N-2}, where v_i are coefficients to be determined. This representation allows us to satisfy each congruence step by step. The algorithm works by iteratively computing v_i such that x satisfies the first i+1 congruences. For each i from 0 to N-1, we solve for v_i using the current partial solution and the i-th congruence. The key step involves solving a linear congruence: (current product of previous moduli) * v_i ≡ (a_i - current_x) (mod m_i). This requires computing modular inverses, which exist if gcd(product of previous moduli, m_i) = 1. If not, the system might have no solution or multiple solutions, and we need to check consistency. The time complexity is O(N² log M) due to N iterations, each involving O(N) multiplications and a modular inverse computation (O(log M)). Space is O(N) for storing coefficients and moduli. Common mistakes include not handling non-coprime moduli correctly, forgetting to reduce intermediate results modulo the product of moduli, and miscomputing modular inverses. To avoid these, always check gcd conditions and use modular arithmetic consistently. Connection to L1: This full solution relies on the key subroutine of solving linear congruences, which is the core of Garner's algorithm. Real code pattern: Initialize x = a_0, product = 1. For i from 1 to N-1: product *= m_{i-1}, compute inv = modular_inverse(product mod m_i, m_i), v_i = ((a_i - x) * inv) mod m_i, x += v_i * product. Finally, x mod total_product gives the solution. Ensure to handle cases where modular inverse doesn't exist by checking gcd.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize x = a_0 = 2, product = 1. Step 2: For i=1 (mod 5): product = 1 * 3 = 3. Compute inv = modular_inverse(3 mod 5, 5) = 2 (since 3*2=6≡1 mod5). v_1 = ((3 - 2) * 2) mod 5 = (1*2) mod5 = 2. x = 2 + 2*3 = 8. Step 3: For i=2 (mod 7): product = 3 * 5 = 15. Compute inv = modular_inverse(15 mod 7, 7) = modular_inverse(1, 7) = 1. v_2 = ((2 - 8) * 1) mod 7 = (-6) mod7 = 1. x = 8 + 1*15 = 23. Step 4: Total product = 3*5*7=105, x mod 105 = 23. Solution: x=23.

Step 1: x = 1, product=1. Step 2: i=1 (mod4): product=1*2=2. inv = modular_inverse(2 mod4,4). gcd(2,4)=2≠1, so check consistency: (a_1 - x) mod gcd = (2-1) mod2 =1 mod2=1≠0, so no solution? Wait, but system has solution. Actually, Garner's requires coprime for inverse, but we can adjust: Since 2 and 4 not coprime, we solve differently. For non-coprime, we check if (a_i - x) divisible by gcd(product, m_i). Here, gcd(2,4)=2, (2-1)=1 not divisible by 2, so no solution? But x=11 works: 11≡1 mod2, 11≡3 mod4? 11 mod4=3≠2. Error in example: Let's use x≡1 mod2, x≡3 mod4, x≡3 mod8. Step 1: x=1, product=1. Step 2: i=1 mod4: product=2, gcd(2,4)=2, (3-1)=2 divisible by 2, so we can proceed by dividing. Compute v_1 = ((3-1)/2) * inv(2/2 mod (4/2)) but simpler: Use Garner's with coprime: Actually, moduli not coprime, so we first combine congruences. But for Garner's, we need moduli pairwise coprime? Garner's typically requires coprime for inverse existence. So adjust example to coprime: Use x≡2 mod3, x≡3 mod5, x≡5 mod7. Step 1: x=2, product=1. Step 2: i=1 mod5: product=3, inv=mod_inv(3,5)=2, v_1=((3-2)*2)mod5=2, x=2+2*3=8. Step 3: i=2 mod7: product=15, inv=mod_inv(15,7)=1, v_2=((5-8)*1)mod7=(-3)mod7=4, x=8+4*15=68. Total product=105, x mod105=68. Solution: x=68. But to match output, use first example. So for non-coprime, Garner's may fail; we need to handle by combining congruences. So for this ladder, assume moduli are pairwise coprime. Use example: x≡2 mod3, x≡3 mod5, x≡2 mod7 gives x=23 as above.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Solve Linear Congruence a*x ≡ b (mod m)
ladders.desc: Given integers a, b, m, find x such that a*x ≡ b (mod m), which is essential for Garner's algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine solves a linear congruence, a core step in Garner's algorithm where we need to find coefficient v_i such that (product) * v_i ≡ (a_i - current_x) (mod m_i). The intuition is to reduce the problem to finding a modular inverse. If gcd(a, m) = 1, then a has an inverse modulo m, and x ≡ b * a^{-1} (mod m). If gcd(a, m) = d > 1, then solutions exist only if d divides b. In that case, we divide the congruence by d to get (a/d)*x ≡ (b/d) (mod m/d), which has a unique solution modulo m/d since gcd(a/d, m/d)=1. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Compute d = gcd(a, m). 2. If d does not divide b, return no solution. 3. Otherwise, reduce: a' = a/d, b' = b/d, m' = m/d. 4. Compute the modular inverse inv of a' modulo m' using extended Euclidean algorithm. 5. Then x0 ≡ b' * inv (mod m') is a solution modulo m'. 6. The general solution is x = x0 + k*m' for integer k, but for Garner's, we take the smallest non-negative solution modulo m'. Time complexity is O(log min(a, m)) for gcd and inverse computation. Space is O(1). Common mistakes include not checking divisibility by gcd, forgetting to reduce modulo m', and miscomputing the inverse. To avoid, always compute gcd first and handle cases carefully. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called in each iteration of Garner's algorithm to find v_i. Real code pattern: def solve_linear_congruence(a, b, m): d = gcd(a, m); if b % d != 0: return None; a //= d; b //= d; m //= d; inv = mod_inverse(a, m); return (b * inv) % m.

examples.trace: Step 1: gcd(3,5)=1. Step 2: 1 divides b? Yes. Step 3: Since gcd=1, compute inv of 3 mod5: 3*2=6≡1 mod5, so inv=2. Step 4: x ≡ 1*2 ≡ 2 mod5. Solution: x=2.

Step 1: gcd(6,8)=2. Step 2: Check if 2 divides b=2: Yes. Step 3: Reduce: a'=6/2=3, b'=2/2=1, m'=8/2=4. Step 4: Compute inv of 3 mod4: 3*3=9≡1 mod4, so inv=3. Step 5: x0 ≡ 1*3 ≡ 3 mod4. Step 6: Solutions are x = 3 + k*4. Smallest non-negative modulo 8: x=3 or 7? But for Garner's, we take modulo m'=4, so x=3. Solution: x=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Mixed Radix Representation of Integers
ladders.desc: Express an integer x in mixed radix form: x = v_0 + v_1*m_0 + v_2*m_0*m_1 + ... + v_{k-1}*m_0*m_1*...*m_{k-2}, which is the foundation of Garner's algorithm.
ladders.explanation: Mixed radix representation is a way to write an integer x using a set of radices (bases) m_0, m_1, ..., m_{k-1}. It generalizes standard positional numeral systems (like decimal) where each digit has its own base. The intuition is that by choosing the radices as the moduli from the congruences, we can build x step by step to satisfy each congruence. Specifically, if we have moduli m_0, m_1, ..., m_{N-1}, we express x as x = v_0 + v_1*m_0 + v_2*m_0*m_1 + ... + v_{N-1}*m_0*m_1*...*m_{N-2}, where 0 ≤ v_i < m_i. This representation is unique for x in the range [0, M-1] where M = m_0*m_1*...*m_{N-1}. The algorithm to compute v_i from x is straightforward: v_0 = x mod m_0, then for i=1 to N-1: x = (x - v_{i-1}) / m_{i-1}, v_i = x mod m_i. Conversely, to compute x from v_i, we use the formula above. The time complexity to convert between x and mixed radix is O(N) if we precompute products. Space is O(N) for storing v_i. Common mistakes include mixing up the order of radices, not reducing v_i modulo m_i, and off-by-one errors in indices. To avoid, carefully define the representation and iterate in correct order. Connection to L0: Garner's algorithm uses this representation to find v_i such that x satisfies all congruences. Real code pattern: To compute v from x: v = []; for i in range(N): v.append(x % m[i]); x //= m[i]. To compute x from v: x = 0; product = 1; for i in range(N): x += v[i] * product; product *= m[i].

ladders.title: Building Block — Modular Inverse Computation
ladders.desc: Given integers a and m with gcd(a,m)=1, find x such that a*x ≡ 1 (mod m), which is needed to solve linear congruences.
ladders.explanation: The modular inverse of a modulo m is an integer x such that a*x ≡ 1 (mod m). It exists if and only if gcd(a, m) = 1. The intuition is that in modular arithmetic, division is not defined, but multiplying by the inverse serves as division. For example, if we want to solve a*x ≡ b (mod m), we can multiply both sides by the inverse of a to get x ≡ b * a^{-1} (mod m). The standard algorithm to compute the modular inverse is the extended Euclidean algorithm, which finds integers x and y such that a*x + m*y = gcd(a,m). If gcd(a,m)=1, then a*x + m*y = 1, so reducing modulo m gives a*x ≡ 1 (mod m), thus x is the inverse modulo m. Step-by-step: 1. Use extended Euclidean algorithm to compute gcd and coefficients x, y. 2. If gcd ≠ 1, return no inverse. 3. Otherwise, the inverse is x mod m (ensure it's positive). Time complexity is O(log min(a, m)). Space is O(1). Common mistakes include not checking gcd, not reducing the inverse modulo m, and handling negative results. To avoid, always compute gcd first and adjust x to be in [0, m-1]. Connection to L1: This is used in solving linear congruences when gcd(a,m)=1. Real code pattern: def mod_inverse(a, m): g, x, y = extended_gcd(a, m); if g != 1: return None; return x % m.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute extended gcd(3,5). 5 = 1*3 + 2, 3 = 1*2 + 1, 2 = 2*1 + 0. Back-substitute: 1 = 3 - 1*2 = 3 - 1*(5 - 1*3) = 2*3 - 1*5. So coefficients: x=2, y=-1. Step 2: gcd=1, so inverse exists. Step 3: Inverse is 2 mod5 = 2. Check: 3*2=6≡1 mod5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modular Arithmetic (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication)
ladders.desc: Perform basic arithmetic operations (+, -, *) under modulo m, ensuring results stay within [0, m-1].
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic involves performing arithmetic operations where numbers wrap around upon reaching a modulus m. The intuition is to work within a finite set {0, 1, ..., m-1}, which is useful in congruences and algorithms like Garner's. Key operations: 1. Addition: (a + b) mod m = ((a mod m) + (b mod m)) mod m. 2. Subtraction: (a - b) mod m = ((a mod m) - (b mod m)) mod m; note that if result is negative, add m to make it non-negative. 3. Multiplication: (a * b) mod m = ((a mod m) * (b mod m)) mod m. These operations are associative, commutative, and distributive modulo m. The algorithm is straightforward: for each operation, compute the result in integers, then take modulo m. However, to avoid overflow in programming, we reduce intermediate results modulo m. Time complexity is O(1) per operation. Space is O(1). Common mistakes include forgetting to reduce modulo m, mishandling negative numbers in subtraction, and integer overflow. To avoid, always apply mod after each operation and use modular arithmetic functions. Connection to L3: Modular inverse computation relies on these operations within the extended Euclidean algorithm. Real code pattern: def mod_add(a, b, m): return (a % m + b % m) % m; def mod_sub(a, b, m): return (a % m - b % m) % m; def mod_mul(a, b, m): return (a % m * b % m) % m.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute 7 + 5 = 12. Step 2: 12 mod 6 = 0. Alternatively: 7 mod6=1, 5 mod6=5, (1+5)=6, 6 mod6=0.
Step 1: Compute 3 * 8 = 24. Step 2: 24 mod 5 = 4. Alternatively: 3 mod5=3, 8 mod5=3, 3*3=9, 9 mod5=4.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Congruences and Modulo Operation
ladders.desc: Learn the basic definition: a ≡ b (mod m) means m divides (a-b), and how to compute a mod m.
ladders.explanation: Congruence is a fundamental concept in number theory. We say a is congruent to b modulo m, written a ≡ b (mod m), if m divides the difference a - b. In other words, a and b have the same remainder when divided by m. For example, 7 ≡ 2 (mod 5) because 7-2=5 is divisible by 5. The modulo operation, a mod m, gives the remainder when a is divided by m. It is always a non-negative integer between 0 and m-1. The intuition is that modulo m partitions integers into m equivalence classes, each representing numbers that are congruent modulo m. This is useful in problems like solving congruences because we care about numbers up to multiples of m. To compute a mod m: if a is non-negative, divide a by m and take the remainder. If a is negative, add multiples of m until the result is in [0, m-1]; equivalently, a mod m = a - m*floor(a/m). Common mistakes include confusing congruence with equality, mishandling negative numbers in modulo, and not reducing results properly. To avoid, remember that a ≡ b (mod m) means (a-b) is a multiple of m, and always compute mod to get a representative in [0, m-1]. Connection to L4: Modular arithmetic operations rely on this definition. Real code pattern: In Python, a % m gives a mod m for positive m, but for negative a, it returns a non-negative result if m is positive. Example: -3 % 5 = 2.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute difference: 17 - 5 = 12. Step 2: Check if 6 divides 12: 12 / 6 = 2, remainder 0. So yes, 17 ≡ 5 mod6.

Step 1: Since -8 is negative, add 5 repeatedly until result is between 0 and 4. -8 + 5 = -3, still negative. -3 + 5 = 2, which is in [0,4]. So -8 mod 5 = 2. Alternatively, use formula: -8 - 5*floor(-8/5) = -8 - 5*(-2) = -8+10=2.
